gaps.description: There is no register view anywhere. The closest things are the read-only General Ledger report (app/reports/general-ledger/page.tsx — plain table, no editing, no payee column) and a read-only 200-row transactions list (app/transactions/page.tsx). A repo-wide search for 'register' across app/ pages finds nothing (only bank-review, which is the bank-feed Add/Match screen). QB Desktop users live in the register; this is the single largest UX-parity hole in the domain. Marked critical because the project's stated goal is a TRUE QBD alternative and the register is QBD's primary transaction-entry surface for bank/CC accounts.
gaps.whereToBuild: new app/accounts/[id]/register/page.tsx (or app/registers/), backed by lib/services/journal.ts generalLedger + new inline-edit endpoints in app/api/journal-entries/

gaps.title: Year-end close is a hard posted closing entry, not QB's soft close — multi-year P&L ranges show the closed year as zero
gaps.qbFeature: QBD automatic year-end close: net income rolls to Retained Earnings virtually on the fiscal-year boundary; no closing entry is posted and prior-year P&L reports remain intact
gaps.description: yearEndClose (fiscalClose.ts:159-209) posts a real entry dated Dec 31 23:59:59 that debits all revenue and credits all expense accounts. No report excludes it (profitAndLoss at reports.ts:104-124 has no closing-entry filter, and nothing in the schema marks an entry as 'closing' since sourceRef is dropped). Any P&L or P&L-comparative whose date range spans the closing timestamp (e.g., a trailing-18-months or multi-year comparison) nets the closed year's income/expense to zero; the closed year's GL registers also show large artificial Dec-31 lines. It only escapes the standard single-year P&L because 'to=12-31' parses to midnight, which is accidental. Add an isClosing flag (or persist sourceRef) and exclude closing entries from P&L-type reports, or compute RE virtually like QB.
gaps.whereToBuild: lib/db/schema.ts (is_closing flag on journal_entries), lib/services/fiscalClose.ts, lib/services/reports.ts + reportsComparative.ts (exclude closing entries from income/expense aggregation)

bugs.title: Creating an account from the UI fails with a 500 unless the user types an exact enum subtype
bugs.evidence: app/accounts/page.tsx:85 sends `subtype: form.subtype.trim() || form.type` from a free-text Input (lines 148-154), defaulting to the type name ('asset', 'liability', etc.). None of the five type names are members of accountSubtypeEnum (lib/db/schema.ts:6-12: 'checking', 'savings', 'accounts_receivable', ...), and createAccount does zero subtype validation — lib/services/accounts.ts:55 casts `input.subtype as never` straight into the insert. Postgres rejects the invalid enum literal, which surfaces as a generic 500 'Internal server error' (app/api/accounts/route.ts:20-21). The default path — leaving the optional Subtype field blank — always fails, as does any capitalized or non-exact value like 'Checking'.
bugs.fixSketch: Two-layer fix. (1) UI: in app/accounts/page.tsx, replace the free-text Subtype Input with a Select whose options are filtered by the chosen Type via a SUBTYPES_BY_TYPE map (asset → checking/savings/accounts_receivable/inventory/fixed_assets; liability → accounts_payable/credit_card/long_term_liability; equity → owners_equity/retained_earnings; revenue → sales/service_revenue/other_income; expense → cost_of_goods_sold/operating_expenses/payroll/taxes), defaulting to the first subtype for the selected type and resetting on type change — eliminating the invalid `|| form.type` fallback. (2) Service: in lib/services/accounts.ts createAccount (and updateAccount), validate against the canonical list — `import { accountSubtypeEnum } from '@/lib/db/schema'`; normalize `input.subtype?.trim().toLowerCase()`, and if it is not in `accountSubtypeEnum.enumValues`, throw `validation(\`Invalid subtype "${input.subtype}". Valid: ...\`)` (or map a blank subtype to a sensible per-type default) so the API returns a 400 with an actionable message instead of a 500.

bugs.title: General Ledger register running balance ignores all activity before the 'from' date (no beginning balance)
bugs.evidence: lib/services/journal.ts:296 initializes `runningBalance = Money.zero()` for every account, and lines 257-258 filter journal lines to `gte(date, from)` when a from-date is supplied. There is no opening-balance computation for activity prior to `from`, and no 'Beginning Balance' row. The GL report page (app/reports/general-ledger/page.tsx:113-141, 210-218) exposes the From filter, so running any date-ranged GL on an account with prior history shows a Balance column and 'Closing Balance' footer that are factually wrong (e.g., a checking account with $10,000 of prior activity starts the period at $0). QB Desktop's account QuickReport/GL always shows Beginning Balance.
bugs.fixSketch: In `generalLedger()` (lib/services/journal.ts), when `opts.from` is set, run one additional aggregate query before the detail query: select `accountId, sum(debit), sum(credit)` from journalEntryLines joined to journalEntries, filtered by `companyId`, `status = 'posted'`, `inArray(accountId, accountIds)`, and `lt(journalEntries.date, opts.from)`, grouped by accountId. For each account, compute the opening balance with the existing `balanceDelta(acct.type, sumDebit, sumCredit)` helper, initialize `runningBalance` to it instead of `Money.zero()`, and prepend a synthetic register row (e.g. `entryNumber: null, description: 'Beginning Balance', debit/credit: null, runningBalance: toAmountString(opening)`) — or add an explicit `openingBalance` field to `GeneralLedgerResult` and render a Beginning Balance row in app/reports/general-ledger/page.tsx and the CSV builder. Add a vitest case in lib/services/journal.test.ts: post entries before and after a cutoff, call `generalLedger` with `from = cutoff`, and assert the first row/opening equals the pre-cutoff balance and `closingBalance` equals the all-time balance.

bugs.title: sourceRef is accepted from 20+ posting callers but silently discarded — GL entries have no link to their source documents
bugs.evidence: posting.ts:37-38 documents `sourceRef?: string` as 'link source document for traceability (e.g. "invoice:<id>")' and 20+ services pass it (invoices.ts:472, bills.ts:151, payments.ts:171, payroll.ts:293, deposits.ts:214, fiscalClose.ts:208, etc.), but the insert at posting.ts:119-130 only persists companyId/entryNumber/date/description/reference/status/createdBy — there is no source_ref column on journal_entries (lib/db/schema.ts:79-91). The value is dropped on the floor, so a GL line for an invoice posting cannot be traced back to the invoice, which permanently blocks QB-style drill-down from the ledger to the source transaction.
bugs.fixSketch: Add the column and persist it: in lib/db/schema.ts add `sourceRef: varchar('source_ref', { length: 100 })` to the journalEntries table (or, better for indexed drill-down, split columns `sourceType varchar(50)` + `sourceId uuid` with an index) and generate a Drizzle migration; in postJournalEntry (lib/services/posting.ts:119-130) add `sourceRef: input.sourceRef ?? null` to the insert values. No caller changes are needed since all ~20 callers already pass well-formed "type:id" strings (plus journal.ts's 'manual'). Then surface it: include sourceRef in getEntry/listEntries (lib/services/journal.ts) and the general-ledger report rows so the UI can parse "invoice:<id>" into a link to /invoices/<id> etc. Optionally backfill existing rows by joining each document table's postedEntryId. Add a test asserting that posting an invoice produces a journal_entries row with source_ref = `invoice:<id>` and that getEntry returns it.

bugs.title: Account opening balance sets the cached balance with no offsetting GL entry — chart of accounts disagrees with every report and trips the integrity checker
bugs.evidence: accounts.ts:57 does `balance: toAmountString(input.openingBalance ?? 0)` in createAccount with no journal entry posted (QB posts to Opening Balance Equity). All reports derive from journal lines, 'not cached balances' (lib/services/reports.ts:1-3, accountActivity at :22-46), so an account created via POST /api/accounts with openingBalance shows the balance on the COA page (which renders accounts.balance, app/accounts/page.tsx:279-281) but $0 on the trial balance/balance sheet — and the app's own integrity Check 2 ('Cached account.balance matches GL', integrity.ts:73-127) will flag it as a mismatch forever.
bugs.fixSketch: In createAccount, stop writing openingBalance into the cached balance directly. Insert the account with balance '0.00'; then, if openingBalance is nonzero, find-or-create an "Opening Balance Equity" account (e.g. code 3900, type 'equity') and post a balanced journal entry through the existing posting engine (lib/services/posting.ts postJournalEntry), debiting the new account and crediting Opening Balance Equity when the account is debit-normal (asset/expense) and the reverse for credit-normal types (for a negative openingBalance, flip direction). The posting engine's applyBalanceDeltas then sets the cached balance to the same value the GL derives, so the COA page, trial balance/balance sheet, and integrity Check 2 all agree. Run the insert + posting in a single transaction (ctx.db.transaction) so a failed posting doesn't leave a half-created account, and optionally accept an `openingBalanceDate` (default today) for the entry date. Add a service test: create an account with openingBalance, assert trialBalance includes it, balance sheet balances, and runIntegrityChecks Check 2 passes.

bugs.title: Audit Trail UI before/after diff is always empty — the detail fetch was never implemented
bugs.evidence: listAuditLogs intentionally omits oldValues/newValues (lib/services/auditTrail.ts:21 'undefined when fetched via listAuditLogs (trimmed for list performance)' and the select at :57-72 doesn't include them). The expandable row in app/audit-trail/page.tsx:104-121 has an if/else where BOTH branches do `setDetail(row)` — the comment at :116-119 admits 'For a proper detail endpoint that would be /api/audit-trail/:id', but no such route exists (app/api/audit-trail contains only route.ts, list-only) and getAuditLog (auditTrail.ts:89) is dead code with no API consumer. Result: the condition at :190 (`oldValues == null && newValues == null`) is always true and the QB-style before/after change detail never renders for any audit record.
bugs.fixSketch: Add a detail route at app/api/audit-trail/[id]/route.ts whose GET handler mirrors the existing list route's error handling but calls the already-implemented-and-tested getAuditLog(ctx, params.id) from lib/services/auditTrail.ts and returns the row as JSON (ServiceError NOT_FOUND maps to 404, as in the existing errorResponse helper). Then in app/audit-trail/page.tsx replace the bogus else branch in toggleExpand (lines 115-121) with `const full = await api.get<AuditLogRow>(`/api/audit-trail/${row.id}`); setDetail(full);` keeping the existing setLoading/toast error handling — the rendering code at :146-207 (shownDetail, JsonDiff grid, llmReasoning block) already works correctly once detail actually contains oldValues/newValues. Optionally add a route test or a page-level test asserting the expanded row renders Before/After for an update entry, reusing the fixtures from lib/services/auditTrail.test.ts.

bugs.title: Closed fiscal periods can never be reopened — reopenPeriod is dead code with no API route or UI, while the error message tells users to reopen
bugs.evidence: reopenPeriod exists at lib/services/fiscalPeriods.ts:40-59, but app/api/fiscal-periods/route.ts exposes only GET (list) and POST (close) — there is no PATCH/DELETE/[id] route, and a repo-wide grep for 'reopenPeriod' and '/api/fiscal-periods' finds no consumer in app/ or components/ (no fiscal-periods UI page exists at all). Yet assertPeriodOpen's PERIOD_CLOSED error (fiscalPeriods.ts:79-82) says 'Reopen it to post.' — an instruction that is impossible to follow anywhere in the product. Closing a period via the API is a one-way door.
bugs.fixSketch: Add app/api/fiscal-periods/[id]/route.ts exporting a PATCH (or DELETE) handler that mirrors the existing route's structure: get the server context via getServerContext(), call reopenPeriod(ctx, params.id) from lib/services/fiscalPeriods, and reuse the same errorResponse mapping (NOT_FOUND→404, VALIDATION→400, else 500), returning the updated row as JSON. Then give the feature a UI surface — e.g., a "Close the Books" section on app/settings/page.tsx or a small app/fiscal-periods/page.tsx that GETs /api/fiscal-periods to list periods, POSTs to close, and offers a confirm-guarded "Reopen" button hitting the new PATCH endpoint (audit logging is already handled inside reopenPeriod via writeAudit). Add a route-level test plus a UI smoke test; optionally also soften the PERIOD_CLOSED message to point at the new screen ("Reopen it under Settings → Close the Books").

bugs.title: updateAccount accepts self/cyclic/cross-company parentId — cyclic accounts silently vanish from the account tree
bugs.evidence: accounts.ts:84 sets `parentId: patch.parentId === undefined ? before.parentId : patch.parentId` with no validation that the parent (a) is not the account itself, (b) is not a descendant (cycle), or (c) belongs to ctx.companyId (the FK at schema.ts:70 only enforces existence, not tenancy). PATCH /api/accounts/[id] (route.ts:39-48) passes the raw body through. getAccountTree (accounts.ts:117-128) pushes a node into its parent's children whenever parentId is in the map, so a cycle (A→B→A, or A→A) means neither node is ever added to `roots` — both accounts silently disappear from any tree-based rendering while still holding balances.
bugs.fixSketch: In updateAccount (and createAccount), when a non-null parentId is provided: (a) throw validation if parentId === id; (b) load the parent with getAccount(ctx, parentId) — this simultaneously enforces existence and ctx.companyId tenancy (404 if cross-company); (c) prevent cycles by walking the ancestor chain of the proposed parent (loop: cur = parent; while (cur.parentId) { if (cur.parentId === id) throw validation('Cannot move an account under its own descendant'); cur = fetch(cur.parentId); } — or fetch all company accounts once into a Map and walk in memory, capped at row count to guard against pre-existing bad data). Optionally also harden getAccountTree defensively: after building the tree, any node not reachable from roots (cycle remnants from legacy data) should be appended to roots so accounts can never silently vanish from the UI. Add tests: self-parent rejected, grandchild-as-parent rejected, cross-company parent rejected, valid reparent succeeds.

bugs.title: Year-end close ignores the company's configured fiscal year end — hardcoded to the calendar year
bugs.evidence: fiscalClose.ts:55-56 hardcodes `yearStart = ${fiscalYear}-01-01` / `yearEnd = ${fiscalYear+1}-01-01` and :201 dates the closing entry Dec 31. Meanwhile the product collects and stores settings.fiscalYearEnd in onboarding (app/onboarding/page.tsx:245-246,299), Settings (app/settings/page.tsx:147-156), and the company PATCH route (app/api/company/route.ts:57) — but a repo-wide grep shows nothing ever READS fiscalYearEnd. A company with a June 30 year end that runs year-end close gets retained-earnings rollover computed over the wrong 12 months.
bugs.fixSketch: In yearEndClose, load the company's settings.fiscalYearEnd (MM-DD string, default '12-31') — either by querying the companies table with ctx.companyId inside the service or by having app/api/year-end-close/route.ts fetch it and pass it in YearEndCloseInput. Interpret input.fiscalYear as the year in which the fiscal year ENDS: parse fiscalYearEnd into month/day, compute fyEndDate = Date.UTC(fiscalYear, month-1, day), exclusive upper bound yearEnd = fyEndDate + 1 day at 00:00 UTC, and yearStart = same MM-DD boundary one year earlier (i.e., yearEnd minus 1 year), which degenerates to the current Jan 1/Jan 1 behavior when fiscalYearEnd is 12-31. Date the closing entry (fiscalClose.ts:201) at fyEndDate (e.g., 2026-06-30T23:59:59Z) instead of Dec 31, keep the CLOSE-${fiscalYear} reference, and handle the Feb-29 edge by clamping the day to the month's last valid day. Add integration tests in fiscalClose.test.ts for a 06-30 FYE company verifying entries dated 2025-07-15 are included in FY2026 close, entries dated 2026-07-01 are excluded, and the closing entry is dated June 30.

bugs.title: Journal Entries page tests status === 'voided' but the enum value is 'void' — voided entries are mis-styled and unlabeled
bugs.evidence: The journal_entry_status enum is ['draft','posted','void'] (lib/db/schema.ts:13) and voidJournalEntry sets status 'void' (posting.ts:191). But app/journal/page.tsx:90 (`if (status === 'voided') return 'danger'`) and :525 (`entry.status === 'voided'`) compare against 'voided', which never matches — voided entries get the neutral badge instead of the danger badge and the 'Voided' action-column label never renders. (The sibling transactions page uses 'void' correctly, app/transactions/page.tsx:12.)
bugs.fixSketch: In app/journal/page.tsx change both comparisons from 'voided' to 'void': line 90 becomes `if (status === 'void') return 'danger';` and line 525 becomes `{entry.status === 'void' && (...)}`. Optionally tighten the badge label at line 510 so 'void' displays as "Voided" instead of the auto-capitalized "Void" (e.g., `entry.status === 'void' ? 'Voided' : capitalize(entry.status)`), and type `status` as the enum union ('draft' | 'posted' | 'void') in the JournalEntry interface so TypeScript would catch this class of mismatch at compile time.

bugs.title: Electron File > New Company / Open Company menu items are dead — no renderer ever subscribes to the menu channel
bugs.evidence: electron/main.js:99-100 sends `mainWindow.webContents.send('menu', 'new-company' | 'open-company')` and preload.js:13 exposes `onMenu`, but a repo-wide grep for 'onMenu' / menu-event handling across app/ and components/ finds zero listeners (only window.bookkeeper.openFile is used, in banking/qb-import pages). Clicking either File-menu item in the desktop shell does nothing. main.js:30 also confirms the per-company data-dir design is unimplemented: 'For now a single "default" company file'.
bugs.fixSketch: Add a small client component, e.g. components/DesktopBridge.tsx ('use client'), mounted once in app/layout.tsx. On mount, if window.bookkeeper exists, subscribe: onNavigate(route => router.push(route)) to make the Reports menu work; onMenu(action) dispatching 'import' -> router.push('/banking') (or '/qb-import'), 'backup' -> call the existing backup API/service then window.bookkeeper.saveFile, 'update-ready' -> toast prompting restart, and 'new-company'/'open-company' -> until TODO 0.9 (multi-company data dirs) lands, route to a company settings/onboarding page or show a "coming soon" toast — or alternatively remove/disable those two menu items in electron/main.js buildMenu so the shell doesn't present dead UI. Also have preload return an unsubscribe (wrap ipcRenderer.on and expose ipcRenderer.removeListener) and call it in the React effect cleanup so listeners don't accumulate across remounts/HMR.

bugs.title: Invoice API silently drops discountType, currency, and exchangeRate that the UI sends
bugs.evidence: app/invoices/page.tsx:157-170 posts { discount, discountType, currency, exchangeRate, ... } but app/api/invoices/route.ts:57-72 forwards only customerId/date/dueDate/taxRateId/discount/memo/lines to createInvoice. lib/services/invoices.ts:280 then defaults discountType to 'amount' and :149 defaults exchangeRate to 1. Result: a user entering a 10% discount gets a flat $10 discount, and a EUR invoice with rate 1.10 is silently posted 1:1 in base currency. The route also drops retainagePercent and per-line taxRateId, so retainage and per-line tax (both claimed done and service-tested) are unreachable over HTTP.
bugs.fixSketch: In app/api/invoices/route.ts POST (lines 57-72), forward the missing fields to createInvoice: add `discountType: body.discountType === 'percent' ? 'percent' : body.discountType === 'amount' || body.discountType == null ? body.discountType ?? null : (reject with 400)`, `currency: body.currency ?? null`, `exchangeRate: body.exchangeRate ?? null`, and `retainagePercent: body.retainagePercent ?? null` (the service already validates exchangeRate > 0 at invoices.ts:150 and retainage <= 100 at :304, so simple pass-through is safe); in the line mapper add `taxRateId: (l.taxRateId as string | undefined) ?? null`. Optionally add a route-level guard that rejects unknown discountType values instead of coercing. Add an API-level regression test that POSTs a percent discount + EUR/1.10 invoice and asserts the stored invoice has discountType 'percent', currency 'EUR', exchangeRate '1.10', and that the GL entry amounts are base-currency-converted.

bugs.title: applyToInvoice (credit memo) recomputes balanceDue from invoice.total, ignoring retainage
bugs.evidence: creditMemos.ts:351 `const newBalance = Money.round2(Money.of(invoice.total).minus(newAmountPaid))` — but invoices store balanceDue net of retainage at creation (invoices.ts:308 `dueNow = total - retainageAmount`, :369) and markPaidAmount correctly uses billedBase = total - retainageAmount (invoices.ts:623-626). Applying a credit memo to a retainage invoice re-introduces the held-back amount into balanceDue: invoice total 100 / retainage 10 / balanceDue 90, apply a 90 credit → newBalance = 100-90 = 10, status flips to 'partial' instead of 'paid' and the customer appears to owe the retainage immediately.
bugs.fixSketch: Mirror the markPaidAmount fix inside applyToInvoice: replace creditMemos.ts:351 with a billed-base computation — `const billedBase = Money.round2(Money.of(invoice.total).minus(Money.of(invoice.retainageAmount ?? '0'))); const newBalance = Money.round2(billedBase.minus(newAmountPaid));` — leaving the status derivation and `Money.abs(newBalance)` write unchanged. Better still, extract a shared helper (e.g. `computeInvoiceBalance(invoice, newAmountPaid)` in lib/services/invoices.ts) returning { newBalance, newStatus } and use it from both markPaidAmount and creditMemos.applyToInvoice so the retainage rule can't drift again; add a creditMemos test applying a credit equal to the net balance of a retainage invoice and asserting balanceDue 0.00 / status 'paid'.

bugs.title: Customer statements omit credit memos, overstating what the customer owes
bugs.evidence: customerStatement builds its ledger only from invoices and paymentsReceived (statements.ts:113-167; opening balance at :78-111 likewise sums only prior invoices minus prior payments). Credit memos credit A/R in the GL at creation (creditMemos.ts:212-217) but never appear on the statement, so openingBalance, runningBalance, and closingBalance are all overstated for any customer with a credit memo. The statement PDF (lib/pdf/statement.ts) renders this wrong data.
bugs.fixSketch: In customerStatement, add credit memos as a third source alongside invoices and payments. For the opening balance, subtract SUM(creditMemos.total) for the customer where status != 'void' and date < range.from (mirroring the prior-invoice query at statements.ts:80-92). For in-range lines, select date/memoNumber/total from creditMemos (non-void, same date-window conditions as invoices at :114-120), map them into the merged RawLine array as kind 'credit_memo', and in the running-balance fold (:173-186) treat them like payments: running = running.minus(total). Use the memo's full total dated at creation — not application events — because that is when A/R is credited in the GL (creditMemos.ts:212-225) and applications post nothing, so this stays double-count-free. Extend StatementLine.type to 'invoice' | 'payment' | 'credit_memo', label the ref as the memo number, update lib/pdf/statement.ts to render the new row type (e.g., "Credit Memo #N" in the credit column), and add statements.test.ts cases covering a prior-period memo (opening balance) and an in-period memo (running/closing balance), both applied and unapplied.

bugs.title: receivePayment has no foreign-currency handling — A/R never clears in the GL for FX invoices
bugs.evidence: createInvoice debits A/R at total*exchangeRate in base currency (invoices.ts:407-431), but receivePayment posts Cr A/R at the raw input amount with no exchange-rate concept anywhere (payments.ts:166-176; ReceivePaymentInput at :37-55 and the paymentsReceived table at lib/db/schema.ts:476-488 have no currency/exchangeRate fields). It also compares the payment amount 1:1 against the invoice's transaction-currency balanceDue (payments.ts:142). Paying a 100 EUR invoice booked at 1.10 leaves a permanent 10.00 base-currency residue in A/R and no FX gain/loss entry.
bugs.fixSketch: Add nullable currency (varchar 3) and exchangeRate (decimal 18,8) columns to payments_received via a Drizzle migration, mirror them on ReceivePaymentInput (default: company base / 1.0), and in receivePayment require each applied invoice's currency to match the payment currency (keep the payments.ts:142 balanceDue comparison in transaction currency, as today). In the GL posting (payments.ts:167-176), debit the deposit account at amount * paymentRate (base) and credit A/R at the base value originally booked for the applied portion — amountApplied * invoice.exchangeRate per application (plus unapplied * paymentRate) — then plug the difference with a realized-FX line reusing the conventions already in lib/services/currencies.ts (Cr 4900 Other Income for a gain, Dr 6100 for a loss), so the entry balances and A/R 1200 clears to exactly zero in base currency when the invoice is fully applied. Invoice amountPaid/balanceDue updates stay in transaction currency unchanged; add a regression test paying a 100 EUR invoice booked at 1.10 with a different settlement rate and assert A/R nets to zero and the gain/loss posts.

bugs.title: Estimate and sales-order conversion is not atomic — failure after invoice creation leaves the source document convertible again
bugs.evidence: estimates.ts:267-292: createInvoice runs in its own transaction, then the estimate is stamped 'closed' in a second, separate inTransaction; salesOrders.ts:250-266 has the same pattern with a bare update outside any transaction. If the stamping step fails, a posted GL invoice exists while the estimate/sales order remains open and can be converted again (duplicate invoice + duplicate revenue). The codebase itself acknowledges this hazard — billTimeToInvoice was explicitly written to do both steps 'in ONE transaction' (timeTracking.ts:304-322).
bugs.fixSketch: Mirror billTimeToInvoice (timeTracking.ts:306-322): in both estimates.ts:convertToInvoice and salesOrders.ts:convertToInvoice, wrap the entire convert in a single `inTransaction(ctx, async (tx) => { ... })` — re-load and re-check the source document's status/convertedInvoiceId inside the transaction (closing the read-then-write race too), call `createInvoice(tx, ...)` with the transaction context (its internal inTransaction nests as a savepoint, exactly as in billTimeToInvoice), then stamp status='closed'/convertedInvoiceId and writeAudit on the same tx, and return the invoice. That makes invoice posting and source-document stamping commit or roll back together. Optionally, for belt-and-suspenders against concurrent converts, add a partial unique index on converted_invoice_id (and/or a `WHERE status <> 'closed'` conditional update whose rowCount is checked) — the same hardening AUDIT_REMEDIATION.md already plans for deferred item #5 (PO→Bill).

bugs.title: listInvoices/listCreditMemos fetch the entire table and filter in JavaScript
bugs.evidence: invoices.ts:504-521 selects all invoices for the company and applies customerId/status filters with rows.filter() in JS (the comment admits 'Drizzle doesn't support dynamic .where chaining easily'); creditMemos.ts:255-270 does the same. With the project's stated 100k+ transaction goal this makes the invoice list O(table size) per request; the filters belong in the WHERE clause (and() / inArray are already imported and used elsewhere in the same file).
bugs.fixSketch: In both listInvoices (lib/services/invoices.ts:504) and listCreditMemos (lib/services/creditMemos.ts:255), build the conditions array up front and pass a single and(): `const conds = [eq(invoices.companyId, ctx.companyId)]; if (opts?.customerId) conds.push(eq(invoices.customerId, opts.customerId)); if (opts?.status) conds.push(eq(invoices.status, opts.status)); return ctx.db.select().from(invoices).where(and(...conds)).orderBy(invoices.invoiceNumber);` — `and` and `eq` are already imported in invoices.ts (line 26; add to creditMemos.ts imports if missing), and Drizzle's and() handles a 1-element array fine, so the JS filter and the misleading comment can be deleted with no behavior change. The existing tests (invoices.test.ts "listInvoices returns invoices scoped to company", creditMemos.test.ts:154) cover the unfiltered path; add one test per service asserting a customerId+status-filtered call returns only matching rows. The same pattern also appears in lib/services/inventory.ts:362, payroll.ts:98/150, and timeTracking.ts:71 and is worth fixing in the same pass.

bugs.title: PO-to-bill conversion is not atomic — double-conversion can double-post A/P
bugs.evidence: purchaseOrders.ts:277-301 — convertToBill calls createBill(ctx, ...) which commits in its OWN transaction (bills.ts:99 inTransaction), then stamps the PO (status='closed', convertedBillId) in a separate later write. A crash/error between the two leaves a posted bill + A/P GL entry with the PO still 'open', and re-running 'convert' passes the guard at lines 258-263 and creates a second bill/second A/P posting. AUDIT_REMEDIATION.md:64-65 acknowledges this (#5) as deferred — still present in code.
bugs.fixSketch: Wrap the entire conversion in one transaction and make the stamp a guarded conditional write. Concretely: in `convertToBill`, after the initial validation, do `return inTransaction(ctx, async (tx) => { ... })`; inside the tx, first execute the stamp as a conditional UPDATE — `update(purchaseOrders).set({status:'closed', convertedBillId: <placeholder or post-set>}).where(and(eq(id), eq(companyId), eq(status,'open'), isNull(convertedBillId))).returning()` — and throw CONFLICT if zero rows came back (this doubles as a row lock/idempotency guard against concurrent converts); then create the bill by passing the tx ctx into the bill-creation logic. Since `createBill` unconditionally opens its own transaction, either rely on Drizzle/PGlite nested-transaction savepoint semantics (calling `inTransaction` with a tx ctx) or, cleaner, extract `createBill`'s body into `createBillTx(txCtx, input)` and have both `createBill` and `convertToBill` call it; finally set `convertedBillId = bill.id` on the PO inside the same tx and write the audit row. As defense-in-depth (the deferred-item note already suggests this), add a migration with a unique index on `purchase_orders.converted_bill_id` so a second conversion can never silently double-post even if a future code path bypasses the guard.

bugs.title: Applying a vendor credit inflates bills.amountPaid, conflating credits with cash paid
bugs.evidence: vendorCredits.ts:291 — applyToBill computes newAmountPaid = bill.amountPaid + applyAmount and writes it to bills.amountPaid (line 302). amountPaid is elsewhere treated as cash payments: voidBill (bills.ts:229-233) blocks voiding with 'Cannot void a bill that has payments applied' even when only a credit was applied, and any consumer reading amountPaid as cash overstates payments. There is no amountCredited column (acknowledged as deferred #44 in AUDIT_REMEDIATION.md:69 but still wrong in code).
bugs.fixSketch: Add an amountCredited numeric column to the bills table (Drizzle migration, default '0.00'). In vendorCredits.applyToBill, increment bills.amountCredited instead of bills.amountPaid, computing balanceDue = total − amountPaid − amountCredited and deriving status ('paid' when balanceDue is zero, 'partial' when amountPaid + amountCredited > 0). In bills.voidBill, keep the payments guard on amountPaid but add a separate guard/message for amountCredited > 0 ('Unapply vendor credits first'). Add an unapplyFromBill(ctx, {vendorCreditId, billId, amount}) function in vendorCredits.ts that, in one transaction, restores credit.unapplied (re-opening the credit status) and decrements bills.amountCredited / increments balanceDue — this makes the existing 'Unapply first' instructions in voidBill and voidVendorCredit actually satisfiable. Update vendorCredits.test.ts:161 to assert amountCredited instead of amountPaid, and add tests for unapply and for voiding a credit-applied bill after unapplying. The same conflation exists on the AR side (creditMemos.ts:350-361 writes credits into invoices.amountPaid; invoices.ts:559 has the same void guard) and should get the matching amountCredited treatment.

bugs.title: 1099 totals include credit-card-funded bill payments (IRS says exclude; QB Desktop excludes them)
bugs.evidence: statements.ts:233-251 — vendor1099Report sums billPaymentApplications joined to billPayments with no filter on billPayments.method or the payment account type, yet payBills explicitly supports method 'credit_card' and credit-card funding accounts (billPayments.ts:37, 127-134). Card-settled payments are reportable on the processor's 1099-K and must be excluded from 1099-NEC box 1; this report (and the e-file XML built on it in form1099Xml.ts:78) overstates nonemployee compensation for any vendor paid by card.
bugs.fixSketch: In vendor1099Report, exclude card-settled payments from both legs. For the bill-payment query, add ne(billPayments.method, 'credit_card') to the where clause, and additionally left-join accounts on billPayments.paymentAccountId to also exclude rows where the funding account is a credit-card liability (accounts.type = 'liability' AND accounts.subtype = 'credit_card'), catching card-funded payments recorded with method 'other'/'ach'. Apply the same two-part exclusion to the direct-expense query (expenses.method / expenses.paymentAccountId per schema.ts:537-540). Because form1099Xml.ts and both report routes consume vendor1099Report, fixing it in one place corrects the report, PDF, and e-file XML simultaneously. Add tests: a 1099 vendor paid $600+ by check appears; the same vendor paid by credit_card method (or from a credit-card account with method 'other') does not; mixed payments count only the non-card portion against the $600 threshold. Optionally surface an "excluded card payments" column like QB Desktop's 1099 wizard for user transparency.

bugs.title: Recurring bill/invoice templates freeze the document date instead of using the run date
bugs.evidence: recurring.ts:91-99 (toBillInput, same pattern in toInvoiceInput:81-89) — 'const date = payload.date ? new Date(payload.date) : runDate'. CreateBillInput.date is a required field (bills.ts:44), so a template payload built from a real create-input carries a date, and EVERY generated recurrence posts on that same original date (dueDate likewise never advances). Only a UI hint mitigates it (app/recurring/page.tsx:276 'omit the date — it is set automatically'); the service/API does not strip it, so monthly bills can silently all post into one historical period.
bugs.fixSketch: In recurring.ts, make the run date authoritative: in toInvoiceInput/toBillInput/toManualEntryInput, set `date: runDate` unconditionally (drop the `payload.date ? ...` branch), and recompute dueDate by carrying the template's original date->dueDate offset forward — `const offsetMs = payload.date && payload.dueDate ? new Date(payload.dueDate).getTime() - new Date(payload.date).getTime() : null; const dueDate = offsetMs !== null ? new Date(runDate.getTime() + offsetMs) : null;` (or, simpler, derive dueDate from customer/vendor terms and otherwise null). Optionally also strip `date`/`dueDate` from the payload in createTemplate so stored templates are canonical, and fix app/recurring/page.tsx:122 to stop injecting `date: form.nextRunDate` into the guided invoice payload. Add a regression test that runs the same template twice via runDue with asOf one month apart and asserts the two generated documents carry the two distinct run dates.

bugs.title: listBillPayments returns OLDEST-first with a default limit of 100, contradicting its documented contract
bugs.evidence: billPayments.ts:297-316 — doc comment says 'Returns payments newest-first (by date desc, then createdAt desc)' but the query is .limit(opts?.limit ?? 100).orderBy(billPayments.date, billPayments.createdAt) — ascending, no desc(). Once a company exceeds 100 payments, the default GET /api/bill-payments response silently omits the most recent payments entirely (limit applied to the ascending set).
bugs.fixSketch: In lib/services/billPayments.ts, add `desc` to the drizzle-orm import on line 14 (`import { and, desc, eq } from 'drizzle-orm';`) and change line 316 to `.orderBy(desc(billPayments.date), desc(billPayments.createdAt))` so the query matches the documented newest-first contract; with descending order the default limit of 100 then correctly returns the 100 most recent payments instead of the 100 oldest. Add a regression test in lib/services/billPayments.test.ts that creates payments on distinct dates and asserts the first returned row is the most recent (and optionally that a limit of 1 returns the newest payment).

bugs.title: GL descriptions/memos for bills and bill payments embed the vendor UUID instead of the vendor name
bugs.evidence: billPayments.ts:210 description: `Bill payment — ${vendor.id}` and :217 memo: `A/P payment to vendor ${input.vendorId}` (the vendor select at line 111-114 fetches only { id }); same in bills.ts:142 memo: `Bill ${...} — ${vendor.id}` and salesTax agency in liabilityPayments.ts:93 (`agency: ${input.agencyId}`). The journal/GL register permanently displays raw UUIDs where QB shows the payee name.
bugs.fixSketch: Widen the existing ownership-check selects to also fetch the display name and interpolate it instead of the UUID. In billPayments.ts change line 112 to `.select({ id: vendors.id, name: vendors.name })`, then line 210 becomes `description: `Bill payment — ${vendor.name}`` and line 217 `memo: `A/P payment to vendor ${vendor.name}`` (keep `sourceRef: vendor:${input.vendorId}` for machine traceability — that field is the right home for the UUID). Do the same in bills.ts (line 75 select add `name: vendors.name`; line 142 memo becomes `Bill ${input.billNumber ?? bill.id} — ${vendor.name}`). In liabilityPayments.ts paySalesTax, when `input.agencyId` is provided and no memo given, look up the agency name from `taxAgencies` (schema.ts:379) scoped to ctx.companyId and use `Pay Sales Tax (${agency.name})`, falling back to the current string only if the lookup misses. Add/adjust unit tests asserting the posted journal description contains the vendor/agency name and not a UUID.

bugs.title: Credit-card (liability) reconciliation math is sign-inverted — can never balance against a normal statement
bugs.evidence: bankAccounts.ts:46-48 explicitly allows linking a bank account to a liability GL account ('A bank account must map to an asset or liability GL account'), but reconcile.ts:137-143 computes the cleared balance as debit − credit with the comment 'For a bank (asset) account the natural-side amount is debit − credit' regardless of account type. For a credit card, charges are CREDITS to the liability, so cleared balance comes out negative while the statement ending balance the user types is positive (amount owed). completeReconciliation (reconcile.ts:458-473) requires statement − cleared ≈ 0, so a credit-card reconciliation can only ever complete if the user knows to enter the statement balance negated. lastReconciledBalance seeding (reconcile.ts:106-117) compounds the sign confusion on the next session.
bugs.fixSketch: In reconcile.ts, make computeClearedBalance account-type aware: when loading the bank account (it already queries bankAccounts at lines 112-115), join accounts on bankAccounts.accountId to fetch accounts.type, then accumulate per-item on the natural side — `debit − credit` for asset, `credit − debit` for liability (e.g., `const sign = glType === 'liability' ? -1 : 1; cleared = cleared.plus(d.minus(c).times(sign))` or equivalent Money ops). The opening seed needs no flip: lastReconciledBalance is stored as the statement balance (statement convention, positive = owed for a credit card), and with credit−debit movements the identity opening + clearedMovements = statementEnding holds in positive statement terms for both account types. Optionally update the StartReconciliationInput docstring (line 41) and add a UI hint on app/reconcile/page.tsx that credit-card balances are entered as the positive amount owed, plus an integration test reconciling a liability-mapped bank account across two consecutive sessions to lock in the lastReconciledBalance seeding behavior.

bugs.title: Reconciled transactions can be voided/unmatched with no guard, silently corrupting the reconciliation opening balance
bugs.evidence: voidJournalEntry (posting.ts:163-204) checks only company scope and period lock — it never checks reconciliationItems, so a line cleared in a COMPLETED reconciliation can be voided (directly, or via bankCategorize.unmatch at bankCategorize.ts:247-263 which voids the matched entry with no reconciliation check). The next reconciliation seeds its opening from bankAccounts.lastReconciledBalance (reconcile.ts:106-117), which still includes the voided line, so the books permanently disagree with the bank by that amount and there is no detection or repair tool. QuickBooks blocks/warns on editing reconciled transactions precisely because of this.
bugs.fixSketch: In voidJournalEntry (posting.ts), inside the transaction after loading the entry's lines, join reconciliationItems on journalEntryLineId for those line IDs, filtered to isCleared = true and reconciliations.status = 'completed'; if any row exists, throw a CONFLICT ServiceError ("This entry contains lines cleared in a completed reconciliation and cannot be voided") — optionally accept an explicit { force: true } option for an admin override that also writes a prominent audit record and adjusts bankAccounts.lastReconciledBalance by the line's natural-side amount so the next reconciliation's opening stays consistent. Because the guard lives in the shared engine, unmatch, invoice/bill/credit-memo/vendor-credit voids, and the journal-entries DELETE route all inherit it automatically; bankCategorize.unmatch should catch the CONFLICT and surface a clear "unreconcile first" message. As a belt-and-braces detection, startReconciliation could recompute the sum of still-posted cleared lines from prior completed reconciliations and warn when it no longer equals lastReconciledBalance (the QuickBooks "beginning balance discrepancy" check).

bugs.title: OFX parser breaks on OFX 2.x XML files and single-line SGML files
bugs.evidence: parseOFXSgml is strictly line-based (import.ts:115 splits on newlines) and the leaf regex at import.ts:134 (`^<([A-Z0-9_.]+)>(.+)$`) captures everything after the tag as the value. OFX 2.x XML emits `<NAME>ACME</NAME>` on one line, so NAME becomes "ACME</NAME>" (polluted descriptions) and `<DTPOSTED>20240115</DTPOSTED>` becomes an invalid date (parseOFXDate at import.ts:77-88 slices '</'' into the hour). Banks that emit the whole SGML body on one line produce a tree where findBankTranList (import.ts:176-184) returns null, so the import 'succeeds' with 0 transactions and no error (import.ts:202-203 returns [] silently). No test or code path handles closing tags on the same line.
bugs.fixSketch: Normalize the input inside parseOFXSgml before line-splitting, and make the leaf regex closing-tag-aware. First, after stripping the header (import.ts:112-113), insert line breaks between adjacent tags so single-line files become one-tag-per-line: `body = body.replace(/>\s*</g, '>\n<')` (safe for already-multiline files). Second, change the leaf regex to strip an optional matching close tag and handle value+close on one line: replace import.ts:134 with something like `line.match(/^<([A-Z0-9_.]+)>([^<]+?)(?:<\/\1>)?$/i)` so `<NAME>ACME</NAME>` yields value "ACME"; keep the existing bare `</TAG>` close handling (import.ts:126) which now also works for XML containers since the normalization puts each close tag on its own line. Optionally skip `<?xml ...?>`/`<?OFX ...?>` processing instructions explicitly (they already fall through harmlessly). Finally, in parseOFX, treat a string-valued doc.OFX as a parse failure rather than silently returning [], and add three regression tests: pretty-printed OFX 2.x XML, fully single-line SGML, and single-line XML, each asserting the correct transaction count, clean description ("ACME"), date 2024-01-15, and amount -42.50.

bugs.title: Categorization rule 'setPayee' is accepted and stored but never applied anywhere
bugs.evidence: The API accepts setPayee (app/api/rules/route.ts:92) and createRule stores it (rules.ts:92), but applyRules (rules.ts:162-178) returns only rule.setAccountId, and applyRulesToAccount (rules.ts:204-215) writes only suggestedAccountId. Neither importTransactions (import.ts:483-499, inserts payee: null) nor bankCategorize.categorize ever reads the matched rule's setPayee, so the payee-override half of the rules feature silently does nothing.
bugs.fixSketch: Change applyRules (lib/services/rules.ts:162-178) to return the full matched rule (or a narrow `{ setAccountId: string | null; setPayee: string | null } | null`) instead of just the account id. Then update the three call sites: (1) in applyRulesToAccount (rules.ts:204-215), when the matched rule has a non-null setPayee, include `payee: rule.setPayee` in the `.set({...})` update alongside suggestedAccountId (and count the row as updated if either field changes); (2) in importTransactions (lib/services/import.ts:483-500), insert `payee: match?.setPayee ?? null` instead of the hardcoded `payee: null`, and pass suggestedAccountId from `match?.setAccountId`; (3) in demoFeed.ts:46, destructure the account id from the new return shape. Finally update the applyRules tests in lib/services/import.test.ts (lines 353-383) for the new return type and add a test asserting that a rule with setPayee actually writes the payee onto the staged bank transaction during import and during applyRulesToAccount.

bugs.title: Bank-feed categorize is not atomic — GL entry posts before the staging row is flagged, outside any shared transaction
bugs.evidence: categorize() calls postJournalEntry (bankCategorize.ts:134-140), which commits in its own inTransaction, and only afterwards updates bank_transactions.matched (bankCategorize.ts:143-151) in a separate statement. A failure between the two leaves a posted GL entry with the staging row still unmatched; the row then shows 'Review' in bank-review and re-clicking Add (or 'Auto-add Suggested', which swallows per-row errors at bankCategorize.ts:230-233) posts the same money twice. Compare deposits.ts:209-259 and transfers.ts:50-96, which correctly wrap post + record in one inTransaction.
bugs.fixSketch: Wrap the whole mutation in one transaction, mirroring deposits.ts/transfers.ts: in categorize(), after the pre-validation reads, do `return inTransaction(ctx, async (tx) => { ... })` and move the postJournalEntry call, the bankTransactions UPDATE, and the writeAudit call inside, passing `tx` to each — postJournalEntry's internal inTransaction nests as a savepoint, so post + flag commit or roll back together. For concurrency safety, make the UPDATE conditional (`.where(and(eq(bankTransactions.id, txn.id), eq(bankTransactions.matched, false)))`) and throw CONFLICT (rolling back the entry) if zero rows return. As defense-in-depth idempotency, actually persist sourceRef on journal_entries (the column/insert is currently missing despite the input field) and add a partial unique index on (company_id, source_ref) WHERE status <> 'void', so a duplicate bank_transaction:<id> post fails at the DB even if the flag logic regresses. Add a test that stubs the bankTransactions update to throw and asserts no journal entry or balance delta persists.

bugs.title: Deposit GL entries carry a permanent 'deposit:pending' sourceRef that is never backfilled
bugs.evidence: createDeposit posts the journal entry with sourceRef: `deposit:pending` (deposits.ts:215) before the deposits row exists, then inserts the deposit header (deposits.ts:222-232) and never updates the entry's sourceRef to `deposit:<id>`. Every deposit entry in the GL therefore has the same useless traceability link, breaking the source-document drill-down convention used elsewhere (e.g. bankCategorize.ts:138 `bank_transaction:${txn.id}`).
bugs.fixSketch: Inside the same `inTransaction` block in createDeposit (lib/services/deposits.ts), after the deposit header insert at lines 222-232 returns the new `deposit.id`, add a backfill update: `await tx.db.update(journalEntries).set({ sourceRef: `deposit:${deposit.id}` }).where(and(eq(journalEntries.id, entry.id), eq(journalEntries.companyId, tx.companyId)))` (import journalEntries from @/lib/db/schema). Because it runs in the same transaction, the placeholder is never visible outside the tx. Alternatively, mirror bills.ts ordering by inserting the deposit header first with a nullable postedEntryId, posting the entry with `deposit:${deposit.id}`, then updating the header — but the single UPDATE is the smaller change. Also ship a one-time data migration: `UPDATE journal_entries je SET source_ref = 'deposit:' || d.id FROM deposits d WHERE d.posted_entry_id = je.id AND je.source_ref = 'deposit:pending'` to repair existing rows (the postedEntryId column makes the historical mapping fully recoverable). Add a test asserting that after createDeposit, the entry row's sourceRef equals `deposit:<deposit.id>`.

bugs.title: listClearable loads every cleared reconciliation item in the entire database on each call
bugs.evidence: The 'permanently cleared' query at reconcile.ts:322-336 filters only on reconciliations.status='completed' and isCleared=true — there is no filter on the bank account, the company, or the lineIds collected at line 320 (which is the obvious intended use of that variable). Correctness survives only because UUID membership is checked in JS afterwards, but the query is an unbounded cross-company table scan that grows with total history and runs on every reconcile-screen load.
bugs.fixSketch: Add `inArray` to the drizzle-orm import in lib/services/reconcile.ts and include `inArray(reconciliationItems.journalEntryLineId, lineIds)` in the `and(...)` of the completed-items query (lines 330-335), so the DB only returns cleared items for the lines actually under consideration; if lineIds can be large, chunk it (e.g. batches of ~500) to stay under parameter limits, or alternatively scope the query by the reconciliation's bank account (eq(reconciliations.bankAccountId, bankAccountId) plus company id if present on reconciliations) which bounds it without a long IN list. The JS Set filter at line 340 can stay as-is for correctness. A cleaner long-term option is to fold the exclusion into the first query as a NOT EXISTS anti-join, eliminating the second round-trip entirely.

bugs.title: An in-progress reconciliation can never be cancelled or have its statement balance corrected
bugs.evidence: PATCH supports only 'complete' and 'toggleCleared' (route.ts:75-95); there is no DELETE handler, and the service layer (reconcile.ts) exposes no cancel/abandon/update function (grep for undo|reopen|cancel across reconcile*.ts and the routes returns nothing). startReconciliation throws CONFLICT while one session is in_progress (reconcile.ts:200-217), and completeReconciliation requires difference ≤ $0.01 (reconcile.ts:462). So a user who mistypes the statement ending balance is permanently locked out of reconciling that bank account — the only escape is force-clearing wrong lines until the wrong balance matches.
bugs.fixSketch: Add a cancelReconciliation(ctx, reconciliationId) to lib/services/reconcile.ts that loads the row via the existing loadReconciliation guard, throws validation() unless status === 'in_progress', then in inTransaction deletes the session's reconciliationItems rows and the reconciliations row itself (hard delete is safest since listClearable only treats lines as permanently cleared via completed reconciliations; alternatively set status='cancelled' — the status column is a plain varchar(50), schema.ts:125, so no migration is needed) and writes an audit record. Also add an updateStatement(ctx, id, { statementBalance?, statementDate? }) that permits edits only while in_progress. Wire them up in app/api/reconciliations/[id]/route.ts as a DELETE handler (cancel) and a new PATCH action 'updateStatement', add a Cancel button + edit-balance field to the active-session view in app/reconcile/page.tsx, and cover with tests: cancel allows startReconciliation again on the same bank account, cancel of a completed session is rejected, and lines cleared in a cancelled session reappear as un-cleared in listClearable.

gaps.title: Sales and purchase forms are completely disconnected from inventory (no perpetual inventory)

gaps.description: createInvoice (lib/services/invoices.ts) resolves only the income account from an item (lines 185-276) and posts AR/income/tax — it never calls recordCOGS or consumeStock, never checks stock, and never decrements quantityOnHand. Bills (lib/services/bills.ts) post Dr expense/asset by dollar amount only, with no item/qty path into stock. purchaseOrders.convertToBill explicitly maps PO lines to 'accountId + amount (not qty/rate)' (purchaseOrders.ts:275-289), dropping itemId/quantity. creditMemos.ts and vendorCredits.ts contain zero inventory references (verified by grep). The only ways stock moves are the standalone manual endpoints POST /api/inventory (adjust/COGS), /api/fifo, /api/inventory/physical-count, and assembly builds. So selling or purchasing inventory items through the normal QB-style document workflow leaves quantityOnHand, averageCost, FIFO layers, and the COGS account untouched — inventory valuation and the P&L are both wrong unless the user manually re-keys every movement.
gaps.whereToBuild: lib/services/invoices.ts (post-line loop), lib/services/bills.ts, lib/services/purchaseOrders.ts (convertToBill / a new item-receipt flow), lib/services/creditMemos.ts, reusing lib/services/inventory.ts recordCOGS and lib/services/fifo.ts receiveStock/consumeStock

bugs.title: Reorder point is not settable anywhere — reorder report and low-stock alerts are permanently empty
bugs.evidence: items.reorderPoint exists in the schema (lib/db/schema.ts:371) and drives reorderReport/lowStockCount (lib/services/inventoryOps.ts:117, 124-127) and lowStock (lib/services/inventory.ts:362-367), but no write path can ever set it: CreateItemInput/UpdateItemInput omit reorderPoint entirely (lib/services/items.ts:22-52), the create/update routes pass only the service fields (app/api/items/route.ts:55-66), the items UI form has no reorder field (app/items/page.tsx:310-383), and qbImport.ts/import.ts never reference it (grep across repo: only schema, snapshots, and the read-side hits). reorderReport filters on isNotNull(items.reorderPoint) so it returns zero rows for any real company, despite TODO.md:20 claiming 'inventory reorder' done.
bugs.fixSketch: Add `reorderPoint?: string | number | null` to both CreateItemInput and UpdateItemInput in lib/services/items.ts, persist it in createItem's insert (`reorderPoint: input.reorderPoint != null ? String(input.reorderPoint) : null`) and in updateItem's .set() with the same `patch.reorderPoint !== undefined ? ... : before.reorderPoint` pattern used for salesPrice (validate it parses as a non-negative number, else throw validation()). Thread it through the routes: in app/api/items/route.ts POST add `reorderPoint: body.reorderPoint ?? null`, and in app/api/items/[id]/route.ts PATCH add `if ('reorderPoint' in body) patch.reorderPoint = body.reorderPoint ?? null;`. Finally add a "Reorder point" numeric input to the item form in app/items/page.tsx (shown at least when type === 'inventory'), and add a service test asserting that createItem/updateItem round-trip reorderPoint and that an item created through the service (not raw DB insert) shows up in reorderReport once quantityOnHand falls to/below it.

bugs.title: physicalCount bypasses the FIFO costing-method guard and corrupts FIFO-tracked items
bugs.evidence: inventory.ts added assertNotFifoTracked specifically so average-cost paths 'must never operate on an item that is tracked under FIFO' (lib/services/inventory.ts:33-48, AUDIT_REMEDIATION.md:45), but physicalCount (lib/services/inventoryOps.ts:194-320) has no such check: it overwrites items.quantityOnHand to the counted value (lines 289-295) without touching inventoryLayers, and values the delta at items.averageCost (line 205) which is null/0 for FIFO items (fifo.ts never sets averageCost). Counting a FIFO item therefore posts a $0 adjustment and desynchronizes quantityOnHand from the sum of layer quantityRemaining, after which consumeStock availability checks (fifo.ts:218-232) and FIFO valuation no longer agree with on-hand qty.
bugs.fixSketch: Minimal fix: export assertNotFifoTracked from lib/services/inventory.ts (or hoist it into a shared helper in lib/services/_base.ts since it only needs ctx + itemId) and call `await assertNotFifoTracked(ctx, input.itemId)` at the top of physicalCount in lib/services/inventoryOps.ts (right after loadInventoryItem, ~line 203), so FIFO-tracked items get the same clear VALIDATION error ("use the FIFO endpoints") the other average-cost paths give; add a test in inventoryOps.test.ts that seeds an inventoryLayers row and asserts physicalCount rejects. Proper follow-up (so FIFO items can actually be counted): add a FIFO-aware count path in fifo.ts that, for shrinkage, depletes layers newest-first (or oldest-first per policy) and posts the GL at actual layer cost, and for overage inserts a new layer at the most recent layer's unitCost — keeping quantityOnHand equal to sum(quantityRemaining) by construction.

bugs.title: buildAssembly/unbuildAssembly bypass the FIFO guard — FIFO components consumed at $0 without depleting layers
bugs.evidence: buildAssembly costs components at comp.averageCost (lib/services/assemblies.ts:294) and decrements their quantityOnHand directly (lines 300-308) with no assertNotFifoTracked call and no inventoryLayers interaction (unbuildAssembly likewise, lines 415-450). For a FIFO-tracked component, averageCost is null→0, so the assembly is built at zero cost while the component's FIFO layers (and the GL value posted when those layers were received) remain intact — inventory value is double-counted in fifoValuation and the assembly is understated. Same guard that protects inventory.ts (inventory.ts:38-48) is missing here.
bugs.fixSketch: Export the assertNotFifoTracked helper from lib/services/inventory.ts (or move it to lib/services/_base.ts) and call it in both buildAssembly and unbuildAssembly for every BOM component item — and the assembly item itself — before the inTransaction block (i.e., right after the componentMap is loaded around assemblies.ts:276 and assemblies.ts:413), throwing the same validation error ("This item is FIFO-tracked; use the FIFO receive/consume endpoints..."). This makes assemblies refuse mixed costing exactly like adjustInventory/recordCOGS do, preventing $0-cost builds and layer/quantity divergence. A fuller follow-up (optional) would actually support FIFO components by calling fifo's consume to deplete layers and using its returned actual cost as the component cost in totalCost, and on unbuild inserting a new inventoryLayers row at the component's last layer cost — but the immediate one-screen fix is the guard.

bugs.title: FIFO postings ignore item.assetAccountId and always hit account 1300
bugs.evidence: receiveStock resolves the inventory account as accountIdByCode(tx, '1300') unconditionally (lib/services/fifo.ts:96) and consumeStock likewise (fifo.ts:270), whereas the average-cost paths honor the item's configured asset account first: 'item.assetAccountId ?? (await accountIdByCode(tx, "1300"))' (lib/services/inventory.ts:147, 254; lib/services/inventoryOps.ts:228). An item with a custom inventory-asset account gets its FIFO receipts/COGS booked to the wrong GL account, so per-account balances and any per-account valuation tie-out are wrong.
bugs.fixSketch: In lib/services/fifo.ts, capture the loaded item and prefer its configured account, mirroring the average-cost services. In receiveStock, change `await loadItem(ctx, input.itemId);` to `const item = await loadItem(ctx, input.itemId);` and inside the transaction replace line 96 with `const inventoryAccountId = item.assetAccountId ?? (await accountIdByCode(tx, '1300'));`. In consumeStock, do the same with the item it loads (line ~270): `const inventoryAccountId = item.assetAccountId ?? (await accountIdByCode(tx, '1300'));`. Optionally also honor `item.expenseAccountId` for the COGS side instead of hardcoded '5000' for full parity, and update the GL-impact doc comment at the top of fifo.ts. Add a test in fifo.test.ts that creates an item with a custom asset account (e.g., a seeded 1310 account) and asserts receiveStock debits and consumeStock credits that account rather than 1300.

bugs.title: Average-cost valuation report values FIFO-tracked items at zero (and includes non-inventory items)
bugs.evidence: inventoryValuation selects every active item with no type filter and no FIFO exclusion (lib/services/inventory.ts:319-328) and values each as quantityOnHand × averageCost (lines 331-344). FIFO flows sync quantityOnHand on items (fifo.ts:130-137, 291-298) but never set averageCost, so FIFO-tracked items appear in this report with real quantities and $0.0000 averageCost/totalValue — the grandTotal understates inventory and cannot tie to GL 1300 (which does carry the FIFO value). Services/non-inventory/bundle items are also listed, unlike QB's valuation reports which cover inventory parts/assemblies only.
bugs.fixSketch: In inventoryValuation (lib/services/inventory.ts), (1) restrict the query to inventory-type items: add eq(items.type, 'inventory') to the where clause; (2) handle FIFO-tracked items correctly by left-joining/aggregating inventoryLayers — e.g. first query SELECT item_id, SUM(quantity_remaining * unit_cost) AS layerValue, SUM(quantity_remaining) AS layerQty FROM inventory_layers WHERE company_id = ctx.companyId GROUP BY item_id, then in the row-mapping loop, if the item has layer rows, report averageCost = layerValue/layerQty (or label costing method 'FIFO') and totalValue = layerValue instead of qty × averageCost. This makes one report cover both costing methods, ties grandTotal to GL 1300, and mirrors the existing assertNotFifoTracked detection (presence of inventoryLayers rows) without any schema migration. Add a test that receives stock via fifo.receiveStock plus an average-cost item via adjustInventory and asserts the valuation grandTotal equals the GL 1300 balance and that service items are excluded.

bugs.title: unbuildAssembly creates/destroys inventory value with no GL entry when component costs have drifted
bugs.evidence: unbuildAssembly reduces the assembly at its averageCost (qty down, averageCost unchanged — value removed = unbuildQty × assembly.averageCost, lib/services/assemblies.ts:441-450) while restoring component quantities at each component's CURRENT averageCost (value added = returned × comp.averageCost, lines 417-427), and posts no journal entry (documented at lines 5-23 as a zero-net wash). The wash assumption only holds if component average costs are unchanged since the build; after any receipt at a different cost, total item-level valuation changes by the difference while account 1300 stays fixed, so the inventory valuation report permanently diverges from the balance sheet.
bugs.fixSketch: In unbuildAssembly, compute removedValue = unbuildQty × assembly.averageCost and returnedValue = Σ(returned × component.averageCost), then close the gap one of two ways. Option A (keep the no-GL design, QB-style): value the returned components at their proportional share of removedValue instead of at current cost — for each component, treat the return as a "receipt" at unitCost = (componentShare of removedValue) / returnedQty and recompute that component's weighted averageCost (existingQty×existingAvg + returnedQty×unitCost) / newQty, exactly mirroring buildAssembly's weighted-average update; this keeps total item valuation unchanged so no journal entry is needed. Option B (keep current-cost restoration): post the difference delta = returnedValue − removedValue via postJournalEntry — if delta > 0, Dr 1300 Inventory Asset / Cr an inventory-adjustment or COGS-variance account; if delta < 0, the reverse — so account 1300 tracks the new Σ qty × avgCost. Option A is simpler, value-neutral, and consistent with the service's documented single-account approach; either way add a test that receives a component at a different cost between build and unbuild and asserts Σ qty×avgCost is conserved (or that the JE delta matches).

bugs.title: setBom allows circular BOMs (only direct self-reference is blocked)
bugs.evidence: The only structural validation is 'c.componentItemId === assemblyItemId' (lib/services/assemblies.ts:115-119); nothing prevents A listing B as a component while B lists A (or deeper cycles). buildAssembly doesn't recurse so it won't loop, but the data model accepts BOMs that QB rejects, and any future recursive costing/explosion over assemblyComponents will infinite-loop on existing data.
bugs.fixSketch: In setBom, after the existing per-component validations and before the insert transaction, add an iterative reachability check over assemblyComponents: starting from the new componentIds as the frontier, repeatedly query `select assemblyItemId→componentItemId rows where companyId = ctx.companyId and assemblyItemId in (frontier)` (Drizzle inArray), accumulate visited componentItemIds, and stop when the frontier is empty; if assemblyItemId ever appears in the reachable set, throw validation('This BOM would create a circular assembly reference: <assembly> is (directly or indirectly) a component of itself.'). Because setBom replaces one assembly's BOM atomically and the check runs against committed rows for all other assemblies, this prevents both 2-node (A↔B) and deeper cycles; cost is O(BOM-graph depth) small queries, negligible at desktop scale. Add tests: A↔B rejected, A→B→C→A rejected, and a legitimate shared-subassembly diamond (A→B, A→C, B→D, C→D) accepted.

bugs.title: physicalCount accepts non-inventory item types despite its loader's name
bugs.evidence: loadInventoryItem (lib/services/inventoryOps.ts:68-75) filters only by companyId and id — not items.type — so a physical count can be posted against a service or bundle item, writing a quantityOnHand and a GL shrinkage/overage entry for an item that should have no stock. Contrast reorderReport, which correctly filters eq(items.type, 'inventory') (inventoryOps.ts:115).
bugs.fixSketch: In loadInventoryItem (lib/services/inventoryOps.ts:68-75), keep the load by companyId+id but after fetching, throw a clear error when the row is not stock-tracked: `if (row.type !== 'inventory') throw validation(`Physical counts can only be recorded for inventory-type items; "${row.name}" is type "${row.type}".`)` (preferring a VALIDATION error over silently widening the where clause to `eq(items.type,'inventory')`, which would surface as a confusing 404 NOT_FOUND for an item that does exist). physicalCount then automatically rejects service/non_inventory/bundle items before any quantityOnHand write or GL posting; the API route maps VALIDATION to 400 already. Add a regression test in lib/services/inventoryOps.test.ts that creates a type:'service' item and asserts physicalCount rejects with { code: 'VALIDATION' }.

bugs.title: POST /api/payroll silently disables documented auto-withholding by coercing missing taxes to []
bugs.evidence: app/api/payroll/route.ts:50 passes `taxes: body.taxes ?? []` to runPaycheck, but lib/services/payroll.ts:53-58 and 164-183 document that an explicit empty array means 'no taxes' while only an OMITTED array triggers computeWithholding. Result: the service's auto-compute path is unreachable through the API — any client that omits taxes gets a paycheck posted with ZERO withholding instead of computed FIT/SS/Medicare. The route also never forwards body.filingStatus / body.periodsPerYear (supported by RunPaycheckInput at payroll.ts:65-70), so they are dead parameters over HTTP.
bugs.fixSketch: In app/api/payroll/route.ts POST, stop coercing the taxes field: pass `taxes: body.taxes` (leaving it undefined when omitted so the service's computeWithholding path at lib/services/payroll.ts:171-183 can run; clients that genuinely want no taxes still send an explicit []), and forward the two dead parameters: `filingStatus: body.filingStatus, periodsPerYear: body.periodsPerYear` (the service already defaults them to 'single' and 26 at payroll.ts:175-176). `deductions: body.deductions ?? []` can stay or also be passed through since the service defaults it at payroll.ts:185. Optionally validate body.filingStatus against the FilingStatus union and that periodsPerYear is a positive integer, returning 400 otherwise; and add a route/service test asserting that omitting taxes yields nonzero FIT/SS/Medicare lines while taxes: [] yields totalTaxes of 0.00.

bugs.title: 941 worksheet line 5a/5c/6 understates total tax: prints employee withheld amounts and omits the entire employer FICA share
bugs.evidence: lib/pdf/payrollForms.ts:324-335 computes `totalSSAndMedicare = employee-withheld SS + employee-withheld Medicare` and prints it as lines 5a/5c/5d, then line 6 'Total taxes before adjustments (line 3 + line 5d)' at :327-328/341-346. On the real Form 941, 5a/5c are taxable wages x 12.4% / 2.9% (employee + employer shares combined). The data layer feeding it (lib/services/payrollReports.ts:218-228) only sums employee 'tax' paycheck lines, and no employer share exists anywhere in the system, so a user making deposits from this worksheet underpays FICA by ~50%. The mislabel 'employee share' does not fix line 6, which the form defines as the full deposit liability.
bugs.fixSketch: Two-part fix. (1) Make the worksheet compute 5a/5c per the form instead of echoing withholdings: extend getForm941Data in lib/services/payrollReports.ts to also return quarter SS-taxable wages and Medicare wages (it already sums grossPay for line 2; cap SS wages per employee at the $168,600 wage base using YTD gross), then in lib/pdf/payrollForms.ts compute line5a = ssTaxableWages * 0.124, line5c = medicareWages * 0.029, 5d = 5a + 5c, line6 = federalWithheld + 5d, and drop the '(employee share)' labels. (2) Record the employer share at paycheck time so GL liabilities match the deposit: in runPaycheck (lib/services/payroll.ts), when auto-computing taxes, also insert employer_contribution lines ('Employer Social Security' = 6.2% of SS-taxable gross, 'Employer Medicare' = 1.45% of gross — no employer match on the 0.9% additional Medicare) and add Dr 6500/6510 Payroll Tax Expense, Cr 2300 Payroll Liabilities for that amount to the posted journal entry; keep them out of net-pay math since employer_contribution lines don't reduce net. Update payroll.test.ts and payrollReports.test.ts expectations accordingly.

bugs.title: W-2 and 941 aggregations include paychecks whose GL posting was voided
bugs.evidence: lib/services/payrollReports.ts:58-68 (w2Data) and :175-184 (form941Data) sum the paychecks/paycheck_lines tables with no check on the linked journal entry's status. A paycheck's entry can be voided via DELETE /api/journal-entries/:id (app/api/journal-entries/[id]/route.ts:23-27 -> voidEntry), which reverses GL balances (lib/services/posting.ts:162-191) but leaves the paycheck row intact. After a void, payrollLiabilitiesDue correctly excludes the entry (lib/services/liabilityPayments.ts:47-52 filters status='posted') while W-2/941 totals and pay stub lists still count the voided check — the file header's claim that 'numbers always stay in sync with the GL-posted records' (payrollReports.ts:7-8) is false.
bugs.fixSketch: Make both aggregations GL-status-aware without a schema migration: in payrollReports.ts, inner-join paychecks to journalEntries on paychecks.postedEntryId = journalEntries.id and add eq(journalEntries.status, 'posted') to the WHERE in both w2Data (lines 58-68) and form941Data (lines 175-184); since createPaycheck always stamps postedEntryId in the same transaction, an inner join on status='posted' cleanly drops voided checks (paycheckLines are already scoped by the filtered paycheckIds, so they need no change). Apply the same filter to the pay-stub listing query. Additionally, harden the void path: in voidJournalEntry (or voidEntry), detect sourceRef LIKE 'paycheck:%' and either write an audit-visible marker / proper voidPaycheck flow or at minimum allow it knowingly — the report-side filter is the essential fix. Add a regression test: create a paycheck, void its entry via voidEntry, assert w2Data and form941Data return zeros and payrollLiabilitiesDue agrees.

bugs.title: Timezone mismatch: pay dates stored as UTC midnight but W-2/941 period ranges built in server-local time, misclassifying boundary-day paychecks
bugs.evidence: app/api/payroll/route.ts:46 parses the UI's 'YYYY-MM-DD' pay date with `new Date(body.payDate)` (UTC midnight), but payrollReports.ts:54-55 (`new Date(input.year, 0, 1)`) and :154-158 (quarterRange) build range bounds in server-local time. On any machine west of UTC (all US timezones), a paycheck dated Jan 1 is stored at 2026-01-01T00:00Z which is < the local-time yearStart (e.g. 2026-01-01T05:00Z), so first-day-of-quarter/year paychecks fall into the PRIOR year's W-2 and prior quarter's 941.
bugs.fixSketch: Build the report range bounds in UTC to match how pay dates are stored: in lib/services/payrollReports.ts replace `new Date(input.year, 0, 1)` / `new Date(input.year + 1, 0, 1)` (lines 54-55) with `new Date(Date.UTC(input.year, 0, 1))` / `new Date(Date.UTC(input.year + 1, 0, 1))`, and in quarterRange (lines 156-157) replace `new Date(year, qStartMonth, 1)` / `new Date(year, qStartMonth + 3, 1)` with the `Date.UTC(...)` equivalents. Since every paycheck's payDate enters the system as UTC midnight (route parses 'YYYY-MM-DD' via `new Date(...)`) and drizzle serializes bounds via toISOString, UTC-midnight bounds make the half-open [start, end) ranges classify boundary-day paychecks correctly in every server timezone. Add regression tests with payDate `new Date('2024-01-01')` and `new Date('2024-04-01')` asserting they fall in year 2024/Q1-vs-Q2 correctly (ideally run with TZ=America/New_York). Optionally audit other report services for the same local-time `new Date(y, m, d)` range pattern against timestamp columns.

bugs.title: Auto-withholding applies SS wage base and Additional Medicare by annualizing the current period instead of YTD wages
bugs.evidence: lib/services/payrollTax.ts:184-197: `annualSSTaxable = min(grossPerPeriod * periodsPerYear, 168600)` and Additional Medicare on `annualGross - 200000`, prorated back per period. There is no YTD lookup anywhere (computeWithholding takes only grossPerPeriod/periodsPerYear/filingStatus). Concrete failure: a $20,000 bonus paycheck on a biweekly schedule annualizes to $520k, capping SS at 168,600*6.2%/26 = $402.05 withheld instead of $1,240.00 (6.2% of 20k) for an employee far below the wage base, and incorrectly triggers Additional Medicare. QB applies both thresholds against actual YTD wages.
bugs.fixSketch: Add an optional `ytdGrossBefore: MoneyInput` (wages already paid this calendar year, excluding the current check) to WithholdingInput. Keep annualization for federal income tax only (that is the legitimate percentage method). For SS, replace lines 186-189 with a per-check YTD cap: ssTaxableThisPeriod = Decimal.max(0, Decimal.min(grossPer, ssWageBase.minus(ytdGrossBefore))); ssPerPeriod = round2(ssTaxableThisPeriod × 0.062). For Additional Medicare, replace lines 193-195 with the portion of this check above the YTD threshold: addlTaxable = Decimal.max(0, ytdGrossBefore.plus(grossPer).minus(200000)).clamp to at most grossPer; medicarePerPeriod = round2(grossPer × 0.0145 + addlTaxable × 0.009). In runPaycheck (lib/services/payroll.ts:173), compute ytdGrossBefore by summing grossPay of the employee's existing paychecks whose payDate falls in the same calendar year as the new check (listPaychecks already fetches them), and pass it through; default to 0 when absent so the /api/payroll/calc estimator route keeps working. Update payrollTax.test.ts:86-93 and 104-111 to assert YTD-based behavior (e.g., $20k bonus with low YTD → $1,240.00 SS and no Additional Medicare; YTD ≥ 168,600 → $0.00 SS) and add a runPaycheck integration test covering wage-base crossover mid-year.

bugs.title: W-2 download picker excludes inactive employees, blocking W-2s for terminated staff
